children.name: Talk Talk — Spirit of Eden
children.reason: Both bands abandoned conventional rock structures for ambient, post-rock soundscapes. Mark Hollis dissolved melody into atmosphere the same way Thom Yorke would a decade later—both reaching toward something more elemental than songs.

children.name: AMM — AMMMusic
children.reason: The British free improvisation collective that taught both Hollis and later ambient musicians how to make silence as important as sound. Their radical approach to collective non-composition directly influenced Spirit of Eden's organic sprawl.

children.name: Koyaanisqatsi
children.reason: Godfrey Reggio's dialogue-free meditation on technology overwhelming nature, set to Philip Glass's hypnotic minimalism. The same visual poetry as OK Computer: time-lapse cities breathing like machines, humans reduced to patterns, beauty and horror inseparable in the dance of progress.

children.name: Ron Fricke — Chronos
children.reason: Fricke's solo follow-up pushes the visual language further—35mm time-lapse cinematography that makes human civilization look like cellular processes under a microscope. Pure visual music about time and entropy.

children.name: Brasília
children.reason: Brazil's modernist capital, designed in the 1950s as a utopian city but experienced as profoundly alienating. The same tension OK Computer explores: rational planning that leaves humans feeling emptier, progress that looks beautiful from above but isolates at ground level.

children.name: Lucio Costa
children.reason: The urban planner who designed Brasília's airplane-shaped master plan. His vision of the city as a machine for living—efficient, rational, inhuman—perfectly captures OK Computer's anxiety about optimization crushing the soul.

children.name: Andrei Tarkovsky — Stalker
children.reason: Three men journey through an industrial wasteland to reach 'The Zone,' where wishes supposedly come true. Tarkovsky's meditation on faith and technology in decay maps directly onto OK Computer's landscape—rusted machinery as spiritual metaphor, progress as slow apocalypse.

children.name: Arkady and Boris Strugatsky — Roadside Picnic
children.reason: The Soviet sci-fi novel that inspired Stalker. Alien visitors leave behind mysterious zones filled with deadly artifacts and wish-granting rooms. The Strugatsky brothers wrote technology as cosmic horror—exactly OK Computer's frequency.

children.name: György Ligeti — Lux Aeterna
children.reason: Ligeti's choral composition for 16 voices creates the same vertigo as 'Climbing Up the Walls'—human voices processed into something alien and sublime. Both works use familiar elements (voices, guitars) to reach toward the inhuman.

children.name: Hilliard Ensemble — Morimur
children.reason: Bach's violin partitas interwoven with Arvo Pärt's mirror compositions—ancient and modern colliding into something that sounds like music from another planet. The same temporal displacement as Ligeti, but through radical simplicity instead of complexity.

children.name: Miles Davis — Bitches Brew
children.reason: The anchor: both albums shocked their core audiences by abandoning familiar structures for something more atmospheric and exploratory. Davis plugged in and went electric; Radiohead unplugged their hearts and went digital.

children.name: Teo Macero
children.reason: Davis's producer who pioneered studio-as-instrument techniques—splicing, looping, treating the mixing board like a compositional tool. His methods directly prefigure how Radiohead and Nigel Godrich would deconstruct rock in the studio.
